claim_text: SSIM is a combined measure of the luminance, contrast, and structure of the image given by^45 SSIM(x, x̂) = l(x, x̂) · c(x, x̂) · s(x, x̂), where luminance l assesses the mean pixel values across the images, contrast c measures the similarity of the standard deviations of the pixel values across the images, and structure s compares the structure.

sub_claims.text: SSIM is a combined similarity measure formed as the product of a luminance comparison l(x,x̂), a contrast comparison c(x,x̂), and a structure comparison s(x,x̂).

evidence.snippet: The system separates the task of similarity measurement into three comparisons: luminance, contrast and structure.

Finally, we combine the three comparisons of (6), (9) and (10) and name the resulting similarity measure the SSIM index between signals x and y ... SSIM(x,y) = [l(x,y)]^α · [c(x,y)]^β · [s(x,y)]^γ (12) where α, β and γ are parameters used to adjust the relative importance of the three components.
In order to simplify the expression, we set α = β = γ = 1 and C3 = C2/2 in this paper. This results in a specific form of the SSIM index (13).

sub_claims.nuance: Eq. (12) gives SSIM(x,y) = [l(x,y)]^α · [c(x,y)]^β · [s(x,y)]^γ. With α=β=γ=1 (the specific form used in the paper, Eq. 13), this collapses to the exact product l·c·s stated in the manuscript.

sub_claims.text: The luminance comparison l assesses/compares the mean pixel values (mean intensities) across the two images.

evidence.snippet: First, the luminance of each signal is compared. Assuming discrete signals, this is estimated as the mean intensity [Eq. (2): μ_x = (1/N)Σ x_i].
The luminance comparison function l(x,y) is then a function of μ_x and μ_y.
For luminance comparison, we define l(x,y) = (2μ_xμ_y + C1)/(μ_x^2 + μ_y^2 + C1) (6).

sub_claims.nuance: Eq. (6) defines l(x,y) solely as a function of the mean intensities μ_x and μ_y, matching the manuscript's wording that luminance assesses mean pixel values.

sub_claims.text: The contrast comparison c measures the similarity of the standard deviations of the pixel values across the two images.

evidence.snippet: We use the standard deviation (the square root of variance) as an estimate of the signal contrast. An unbiased estimate in discrete form is given by σ_x = ((1/(N-1))Σ(x_i - μ_x)^2)^(1/2) (4).
The contrast comparison c(x,y) is then the comparison of σ_x and σ_y.
The contrast comparison function takes a similar form: c(x,y) = (2σ_xσ_y + C2)/(σ_x^2 + σ_y^2 + C2) (9).

sub_claims.nuance: Eq. (9) defines c(x,y) solely as a function of the standard deviations σ_x and σ_y; the paper explicitly calls it 'the comparison of σ_x and σ_y,' matching the manuscript.

sub_claims.text: The structure comparison s compares the structure of the two images (operationalized as the correlation of the luminance-subtracted, variance-normalized signals).

evidence.snippet: Third, the signal is normalized (divided) by its own standard deviation, so that the two signals being compared have unit standard deviation. The structure comparison s(x,y) is conducted on these normalized signals (x-μ_x)/σ_x and (y-μ_y)/σ_y.

Structure comparison is conducted after luminance subtraction and variance normalization... The correlation (inner product) between these is a simple and effective measure to quantify the structural similarity.
Thus, we define the structure comparison function as follows: s(x,y) = (σ_xy + C3)/(σ_xσ_y + C3) (10).

sub_claims.nuance: Eq. (10) defines s(x,y) as the correlation coefficient between the normalized signals — i.e., a comparison of the two images' structure, consistent with the manuscript's phrasing 'structure s compares the structure.'

phrasings_tried: SSIM(x,y) = l(x,y) · c(x,y) · s(x,y) as product of three comparisons
luminance comparison function l is a function of mean intensity μ
contrast comparison function c is the comparison of standard deviations σ
structure comparison s on luminance-subtracted, variance-normalized signals / correlation coefficient
combine three comparisons (6), (9), (10) into SSIM index with α=β=γ=1

section_checklist.section: II. Image Quality Assessment Based on Error Sensitivity
III.C Image Quality Assessment Using SSIM Index

attestation.indirect_attribution_check: Wang 2004 is the primary source for the SSIM definition; no earlier primary is credited for Eqs. (6), (9), (10), (12), (13). A precursor 'universal quality index' (UQI) in refs [6] and [7] is mentioned as the special case C1=C2=0 (which produces unstable results), but the full SSIM formulation cited by the manuscript originates here.
attestation.out_of_context_check: The manuscript cites Wang 2004 to define SSIM within an MRI reconstruction evaluation; Wang develops SSIM on natural images with JPEG/JPEG2000 distortions. The formula is domain-agnostic and the manuscript uses it as a general image-similarity metric, which is standard in-context use of the definition.
